model_narrative: An airline has a fleet of different aircraft types and a set of routes that must be flown. For each route it decides how many aircraft of each type to assign, and how much passenger demand to leave unserved by bumping it. Operating an aircraft on a route costs a known amount that depends on both the aircraft type and the route, and bumping demand on a route incurs a per-unit penalty. The airline wants to make the combined total of operating costs and bumping penalties as small as possible.

sets.name: j
sets.doc: the routes that must be served

params.name: c
params.doc: the operating cost of flying one aircraft of a given type on a given route, in thousands of dollars per aircraft

params.name: pcap
params.doc: the passenger capacity of one aircraft of a given type on a given route, in hundreds of passengers per aircraft

params.name: aircraft
params.doc: the number of aircraft of each type available to assign, as a count

params.name: fixeddemand
params.doc: the passenger demand that must be served on each route, in hundreds of passengers

params.name: costbumped
params.doc: the penalty cost per unit of passenger demand that is bumped on a route rather than served, in thousands of dollars per hundred passengers

vars.name: x
vars.doc: the number of aircraft of each type assigned to each route

vars.name: bumped
vars.doc: the amount of passenger demand bumped on each route, in hundreds of passengers

vars.name: z
vars.doc: the total cost, in thousands of dollars, combining aircraft operating costs and bumping penalties

constraints.name: z
constraints.intent: The total cost brings together everything the airline pays. For each aircraft type and route combination, value the aircraft assigned to that route at its operating cost, and add up these amounts over all combinations. Then add the bumping penalties, charging each route's per-unit penalty against the demand bumped on that route. Set the total cost equal to the sum of all the operating costs plus all the bumping penalties.

constraints.name: x[i,
constraints.intent: Each aircraft type is available only in limited quantity. For each aircraft type, the total number of that type assigned across all routes must not exceed the number of that type available.

constraints.name: pcap[i,
constraints.intent: Every route's passenger demand has to be covered, either by flying enough seats or by bumping the shortfall. For each route, the seating capacity provided by all the aircraft assigned to it, together with the demand bumped on that route, must be at least the demand required on that route.

query: There's a service-quality proposal we're evaluating for route-1: hold its bumped passengers to no more than 10% of route-1's booked demand, so the heaviest run isn't where every denied boarding lands. Plug that in and tell me where total cost ends up.

query: We're evaluating a charter-reserve policy: keep at least 3 type-c airframes off the scheduled network so they're available for ad-hoc charters. If we hold type-c deployment to no more than its availability minus 3, what does total cost come out to?
expected_pyomo: model.c_reserve = Constraint(expr=sum(model.x['c', j] for j in model.j) <= model.aircraft['c'] - 3)

query: Bumping is concentrated on route-1. Couldn't we require that no single route bumps more than 25% of the total bumped passengers across the network? I'd expect a more balanced plan to beat the current one. What's keeping the model from spreading it out?

query: Each route is dominated by one or two aircraft types in the current plan. Wouldn't it be better if no single aircraft type made up more than 60% of the total aircraft assigned to any given route? Shouldn't a more diverse mix be at least competitive? What's the model seeing that makes it lean so hard on one type per route?

constraints.intent: Each aircraft type is available only in limited quantity. For each aircraft type, the total number of that type assigned across all routes must not exceed the number of that type available.

constraints.name: pcap[i,
constraints.intent: Every route's passenger demand has to be covered, either by flying enough seats or by bumping the shortfall. For each route, the seating capacity provided by all the aircraft assigned to it, together with the demand bumped on that route, must be at least the demand required on that route.

query: We're flying the whole fleet flat out to carry nearly every passenger, and that's expensive. Denied boardings cost us a fixed compensation per passenger, and on the long runs that's cheap relative to putting another wide-body in the air. Shouldn't the plan be willing to bump a lot more passengers across the network — say at least 80 in total — and pull some aircraft off the line? What's keeping the model from leaning on bumping instead of flying everyone?
expected_pyomo: model.bump_floor = Constraint(expr=sum(model.bumped[j] for j in model.j) >= 80)

query: Route-1 is carrying a stack of airframes while it's also the one we keep bumping passengers off. Wouldn't it be healthier to cap the airframes deployed on route-1 at 12 so we're not concentrating the operation on a single run? What's keeping the model from thinning out route-1?
expected_pyomo: model.r1_aircraft_cap = Constraint(expr=sum(model.x[i, 'route-1'] for i in model.i) <= 12)
